Closed or Open Architectured Ontologies: The Enterprise Race to Make Data AI-Ready
Tzvi Weitzner
Follow
5 min read
·
Oct 22, 2025

5

A look at how the choice of ontology architecture determines the resources, timeliness, and ROI of making enterprise data truly AI-ready

Press enter or click to view image in full size
Enterprises everywhere are racing to make their data AI-ready, yet few have achieved it.

The barrier isn’t just technology, but meaning. Data stored in warehouses and lakes still lacks the semantic context that allows machines to reason, relate, and act intelligently.

Ontologies, frameworks that define and connect concepts across data, are emerging as the foundation for this next stage of enterprise transformation. But not all ontologies are built alike. Some thrive in closed ecosystems that deliver power through control, while others embrace openness to enable flexibility, interoperability, and innovation.

This article examines how ontology architectures are shaping the enterprise AI landscape, comparing Palantir’s proprietary approach with the rise of open, SQL-native alternatives, and what this means for the future of data intelligence.

Introduction: The New Enterprise AI Divide

Every enterprise today is racing toward the same finish line, making data AI-ready. Yet, despite massive investments in data infrastructure, many organizations still struggle to make sense of their information. Data remains fragmented across warehouses, lakes, and applications, while teams spend months building pipelines only to deliver partial, context-poor insights.

At the heart of this challenge lies a missing layer, the ontology, or unified semantic foundation that gives meaning and relationships to data. In the coming era of AI-driven operations, the question for every enterprise is no longer if they will adopt ontologies, but what kind of ontology architecture will define their future: closed or open.

Ontologies: The Framework for Intelligent Data
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Making data AI-ready means more than cleaning or labeling it. It means enabling systems, both analytical and generative, to understand the context and relationships behind data. Large Language Models (LLMs) and enterprise AI agents cannot reason over raw data tables; they need to grasp how entities such as “customer,” “transaction,” and “region” interrelate.

An ontology provides this missing intelligence. It extends beyond a simple semantic layer by connecting disparate sources through shared definitions, relationships, rules, and metrics. Leveraging inference capabilities enabled by inheritance and transitive properties, ontologies transform data into knowledge, making it discoverable, explainable, and machine-navigable.

This is why modern leaders in AI transformation are pivoting from traditional data integration to ontology-driven architectures. Palantir, for instance, built its multibillion-dollar enterprise around this principle. And now, a new generation of open-architectured ontologies, such as Timbr’s SQL-based model, is expanding this power to the broader ecosystem.

The Palantir Playbook: Ontologies as an Operating System

Palantir’s Foundry platform popularized the concept of an ontology-centric enterprise OS. At its core, Foundry’s ontology abstracts complex technical data into operational objects, aircraft, supply chains, financial entities, that mirror how the business itself operates.

This ontology-driven model has been hugely successful, particularly in industries where data integration and decision-making must happen in real time, defense, intelligence, manufacturing, and logistics. By embedding governance, lineage, and operational logic directly into the ontology, Foundry enables a kind of digital twin of the enterprise, allowing users to analyze, simulate, and act on data through intuitive applications.

Get Tzvi Weitzner’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

However, this model comes at a cost: it is proprietary and closed by design. Foundry’s ontologies are defined within its own object framework, supported by ingestion-based pipelines and custom APIs. While powerful, this approach creates dependencies that bind customers to the Palantir ecosystem.

Deployments are typically service-intensive and costly, with total cost of ownership reaching into millions annually for large enterprises. This closed architecture means that while Palantir users benefit from a highly integrated ontology, they often sacrifice flexibility, openness, and direct access to their own semantic models.

Press enter or click to view image in full size
Timbr and Palantir Foundry Platforms at a Glance
The Case for Open Architectured Ontologies

An alternative vision is emerging, one that keeps the power of ontologies but removes the walls around them. Open architectured ontologies, such as those built on SQL-native models, take a fundamentally different stance: the ontology doesn’t replace your data infrastructure; it augments it.

Platforms like Timbr enable enterprises to model their semantic layer directly in SQL, the universal language of data. This approach creates knowledge graphs natively queryable in SQL, without requiring new languages or frameworks. Because the ontology sits virtually above the data, it eliminates the need for ETL or ingestion.

Open architectured ontologies also embrace interoperability. Timbr, for example, allows data teams to connect directly to relational databases, data lakes, APIs, and BI tools through standard JDBC/ODBC and REST interfaces, while remaining fully portable across clouds and ecosystems.

This open design ensures that semantic definitions, concepts, hierarchies, measures, can be shared across teams, reused across projects, and accessed by any analytics or AI framework. The result is a future-proof foundation for enterprise AI: governed, explainable, and independent of proprietary lock-ins.

Closed vs. Open: Two Paths to the Same Goal

Both closed and open ontology architectures share a common mission: to unify data meaning. Yet, their philosophies diverge sharply.

Closed systems deliver turnkey control and embedded intelligence, but their scalability is limited by ecosystem boundaries. Open systems enable choice and composability, allowing enterprises to evolve their ontology as new AI frameworks emerge.

Press enter or click to view image in full size
Palantir Foundry vs Timbr

In essence, the question becomes: do you want your ontology to define your enterprise, or empower it?

The Future of AI Will Be Built on Ontologies, Open Ones

As the enterprise AI landscape matures, ontologies will define who truly leads in data intelligence. Palantir proved the transformative power of semantic architectures, but also the risks of closed ecosystems.

The next generation of leaders will build on that legacy by embracing openness: ontologies that speak SQL, connect to everything, and serve as the universal layer of meaning for both humans and machines.

The race to make data AI-ready is not about who has the most data, but who can make data understood. And in that race, open-architectured ontologies are already setting the pace.

It’s time to connect the dots. Timbr can help. Let us show you a live demo of what this looks like.
